title: Best Cardio for Belly Fat Loss: Walking, Running, HIIT, and Cycling Compared
introduction: The best cardio for belly fat loss is the cardio you can sustain consistently while maintaining a calorie deficit. This guide compares walking, running, HIIT, and cycling for abdominal fat reduction.

sections.heading: 🚴 Cardio Types Ranked for Belly Fat
sections.content:
| Cardio Type | Pros | Best For |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Walking | High adherence, low recovery cost | Daily consistency |
| Running | High calorie burn per minute | Time-efficient sessions |
| HIIT | Strong metabolic stimulus | Short hard workouts |
| Cycling | Joint-friendly option | Low-impact cardio |

Use [calorie burn calculator](/tools/calorie-burn-calculator) to estimate your actual burn.

sections.heading: How Much Cardio for Belly Fat Loss?
sections.content:
| Goal Level | Weekly Cardio Target |
| --- | --- |
| Baseline | 90 min/week |
| Moderate fat loss | 120-180 min/week |
| Higher-output phase | 180-240 min/week |

Related pages:
• [walking for belly fat loss](/blog/walking-for-belly-fat-loss)
• [does running burn belly fat](/blog/does-running-burn-belly-fat)
• [best exercises for belly fat](/blog/best-exercises-for-belly-fat)

sections.heading: Text CTA: Match Cardio to Calorie Data
sections.content: Text CTA: Use Eati to track cardio sessions alongside calories and protein so your belly fat loss stays balanced and sustainable.

midArticleCta: Track cardio volume and intake in Eati to optimize belly fat loss without overtraining.
conclusion: No single cardio method is magic for belly fat. The best choice is the one you can do consistently while maintaining recovery and nutrition quality.
metaTitle: Best Cardio for Belly Fat Loss: Walking vs Running vs HIIT
metaDescription: Compare the best cardio options for belly fat loss and learn how much cardio per week supports abdominal fat reduction.
targetKeyword: best cardio for belly fat loss

faqs.question: Is HIIT better than walking for belly fat?
faqs.answer: HIIT can burn more per minute, but walking is often easier to sustain frequently. Long-term adherence usually matters more than intensity alone.
